name: Finland
summary: World-leading safety, very high English and a structural IT/AI talent shortage suit two software engineers, and a Finnish MSc unlocks a fast PR route. But visas route through New Delhi, 2025-2026 immigration tightening (6yr PR/citizenship, B1/A2 language, work-history and income tests, citizenship test from 2027) and a Finns-Party-led restrictive government plus a small Bangladeshi community amid high anti-Muslim discrimination weigh against this couple.

links.title: Finnish Immigration Service (Migri)
Study in Finland

job-market.summary: Structural IT/AI shortage (~130k IT hires needed by 2030) but a 2026 downturn with ~10% unemployment limits entry for two at once.
job-market.reasoning: Finland has a recognised long-term shortage in software, AI and cybersecurity, with the tech sector growing ~15% and the country projected to need ~130,000 new IT professionals by 2030, so experienced English-speaking engineers stay in demand. However, the 2026 labour market is soft with general unemployment near 10% and entry-level roles intensely competitive, so placing both spouses quickly carries real friction, easing at senior level (7+ years).
evidence: Finland projected to need ~130,000 new IT professionals by 2030; ~25,000 new tech roles being added (2026).
Acute shortages in AI engineering and cybersecurity; ~90 open ML/AI roles logged (Oct 2025).
General unemployment ~10% in 2026; entry-level roles see ~200 applicants each.
Senior/specialist pool is thin and largely employed, favouring experienced hires.

links.title: Migri – Work in Finland
Hiring in Finland in 2026 (Intelligent Employment)
Top in-demand skills in Finland 2026 (Edstellar)

visa-access.summary: No Finnish processing in Dhaka; Bangladeshis apply on Enter Finland then give biometrics at VFS New Delhi (Embassy of Finland).
visa-access.reasoning: For long-term study/work residence permits, Bangladeshi nationals apply on Enter Finland and then identify and give biometrics at the Finland Visa Application Centre (VFS Global) in New Delhi, since there is no Finnish mission in Dhaka and Finland is represented by Sweden for visas. Document legalisation (birth/marriage certificates) is required for Bangladeshi-origin applicants, and processing of permits typically runs up to 90 days, adding cost, travel and time.
evidence: Embassy of Finland, New Delhi handles Bangladesh; biometrics taken at VFS New Delhi by appointment.
Process: apply on Enter Finland first, then book a VFS appointment in New Delhi.
Bangladeshi applicants must legalise birth/marriage and bachelorhood/spinsterhood certificates.
Permit applications generally processed within ~90 days; VFS service fee plus courier applies.

links.title: Residence permits to Finland – Finland abroad: Bangladesh
Embassy of Finland, New Delhi – Finland abroad: Bangladesh

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship allowed (no renunciation), but 6yr residence (5yr if A2 Finnish/Swedish), income test from Dec 2025, test from 2027.
citizenship.reasoning: From 17 December 2025 the residence requirement rose from five to six consecutive years, reduced to five for applicants who meet the A2 Finnish/Swedish language requirement (the mooted eight years was not enacted). A new sufficient-income rule (no reliance on unemployment/social assistance) applies, and a citizenship test enters force early 2027. Crucially, Finland still permits dual citizenship, so this couple need not renounce Bangladeshi nationality — a major plus that offsets the language and tightening friction.
evidence: Residence: 6 consecutive years standard, 5 years if A2 Finnish or Swedish met (effective 17 Dec 2025).
New self-sufficiency rule: no unemployment benefit/social assistance >3 months in the past 2 years.
Dual citizenship permitted; Bangladeshi nationality need not be renounced.
Citizenship test (20-40 MCQs, ~70% pass, in Finnish/Swedish) to take effect early 2027.

links.title: Migri – Period of residence
Ministry of the Interior – Reform of the Citizenship Act
Finnish Government – citizenship test proposal

post-study-work.summary: 2-year job-search residence permit after graduation, splittable; family member works freely; 3-6mo unemployment grace eases job loss.
post-study-work.reasoning: Graduates of Finnish degrees get a residence permit to look for work or start a business for up to two years (can be taken in parts, applied for up to five years after graduation), and switching to a Specialist/EU Blue Card permit is straightforward for engineers above the €3,937/month (2026) salary floor. A family member on family-ties grounds has an unrestricted right to work throughout, and the unemployment grace period (three months, six for specialists/Blue Card holders or 2-year residents, effective 11 June 2025) softens the risk of job loss voiding a permit.
evidence: Post-study residence permit to seek work/start a business: up to 2 years, splittable, applied within 5 years of graduating.
Specialist/EU Blue Card salary floor €3,937/month (2026); engineers clear it comfortably.
Family-ties permit holder has unrestricted right to work, trade and study.
Unemployment grace: 3 months (6 months for specialists/Blue Card/2-yr residents) from 11 June 2025.

links.title: Migri – Seeking work after graduation or completion of research
Finland – Unemployment Protection Period (Fragomen)
Migri – Income requirement (work)

spouse-family.summary: Both can work freely: family-ties permit gives unrestricted work in study and work phases; or each applies independently as a student.
spouse-family.reasoning: A spouse holding a residence permit on the basis of family ties has an unrestricted right to work, run a business and study throughout both the study and work phases, so the second software engineer can work freely from arrival — no taught-master's dependant work ban as in the UK. Bringing family carries an income requirement, and the government has tightened conditions for students' family members, but the couple can also each apply independently as students, strengthening their position.
evidence: Family-ties residence permit = unrestricted right to work, pursue a trade and study, in both phases.
First family-member permit up to 1 year, extendable up to 4 years (tied to sponsor's permit dates).
Income requirement applies to bring family; conditions for students' family members tightened.
Both spouses can instead apply independently as degree students, each gaining study + work rights.

links.title: Migri – Student's family members
Migri – Income requirement for family members

msc-study.summary: Abundant English-taught CS/AI MSc; non-EU tuition ~€8-20k/yr with waivers; student works, spouse works; €9,600/yr funds proof.
msc-study.reasoning: Finnish universities offer many English-taught CS/AI master's programmes, with non-EU tuition roughly €8,000-20,000/year and widely available 25-100% scholarships/waivers (incl. the national Finland Scholarship). Students may work, accompanying spouses get full work rights, and the FSHS health fee is nominal, but applicants must show €9,600 in funds for the first year (€800/month) on a six-month bank statement.
evidence: Non-EU master's tuition ~€8,000-20,000/year (e.g. University of Helsinki ~€13,000-18,000).
Scholarships/fee waivers of 25-100% common; national Finland Scholarship scheme.
Proof of funds: €9,600 for first year (€800/month), shown on a 6-month bank statement.
Student permit allows part-time work; accompanying spouse gets unrestricted work rights; FSHS fee ~€36/term.

links.title: Study in Finland – Student residence permit
Migri – Income requirement for students
University of Helsinki – residence permit for degree students

pr-pathway.summary: Finnish MSc/doctoral grads have NO residence-period requirement for PR; others need 6yr (4yr if €40k) + B1 + 2yr work.
pr-pathway.reasoning: From 8 January 2026 the standard continuous-residence requirement for permanent residence rose from four to six years, with new B1 Finnish/Swedish and two-year work-history requirements; a €40,000 annual income gives a four-year fast-track. Decisively for this couple, there is no period-of-residence requirement at all for holders of a Finnish master's, licentiate or doctoral degree, so completing their MSc in Finland makes PR fast and predictable once the work-history and language conditions are met.
evidence: No residence-period requirement for PR for Finnish master's/licentiate/doctoral (or bachelor's) graduates.
Standard residence raised 4→6 years; 4-year fast-track if annual income ≥ €40,000 (effective 8 Jan 2026).
B1 Finnish/Swedish and ≥2 years of gainful employment in Finland now required.
Amendments signed by the President 22 Dec 2025; pre-8-Jan applications use the old 4-year rules.

links.title: Migri – Amendments to Aliens Act regarding permanent residence permits 2026
Finnish Government – stricter PR conditions as of 8 January

income-cost.summary: Two software salaries (mid ~€62-72k, senior ~€95k) net ~€3.5-4.5k each vs ~€2,000-2,300/mo costs give solid savings.
income-cost.reasoning: Mid-level software engineers in Helsinki earn roughly €62,000-72,000/year gross (seniors ~€95,000), netting about €3,500-4,500/month after tax; two earners support comfortable savings against Helsinki single-person costs of ~€2,000-2,300/month including a ~€1,050 city-centre one-bed rent. The dual income materially improves savings capacity, though Helsinki is the second-priciest Finnish city and a soft 2026 market tempers upside.
evidence: Helsinki software engineer: mid-level €62,000-72,000/yr, senior (8+ yrs) ~€95,000, average ~€84k gross.
Net pay ~€3,500-4,500/month per mid-to-senior engineer in the capital.
Helsinki 1-bed city-centre rent ~€1,049/month; single-person costs ~€2,000-2,300 incl. rent (Numbeo, Jun 2026).
Two incomes give strong combined savings; housing pressure on small flats due to immigration demand.

links.title: Cost of Living in Helsinki – Numbeo (Jun 2026)
Software Engineer Salary in Helsinki – Levels.fyi

healthcare.summary: Universal tax-funded healthcare via wellbeing counties; high quality; students get cheap FSHS; newcomers may need bridging cover.
healthcare.reasoning: Finland provides universal, publicly funded healthcare to residents through the wellbeing services counties, with strong preventive care and among the world's best life expectancy outcomes. Higher-education students access affordable FSHS care for a nominal per-term fee, though non-EU newcomers may need private insurance until they qualify as municipal residents.
evidence: Universal healthcare for residents, organised by wellbeing services counties (SOTE reform).
Strong preventive focus; among the best life expectancy and low infant-mortality outcomes.
Students access FSHS for ~€36/term (general, mental health, dental care).
Non-EU arrivals may need private insurance until municipal/residency coverage applies.

links.title: InfoFinland – Health services in Finland
Study in Finland – health and wellbeing

culture-language.summary: Very high English (EF #12, score 603) works day-to-day, but A2 needed for citizenship and B1 for PR; Finnish is hard.
culture-language.reasoning: English works well in daily life, tech workplaces and English-taught degrees; Finland ranks 12th globally in the 2025 EF index (score 603, 'very high proficiency'). However, citizenship now requires A2 and permanent residence B1 Finnish/Swedish, and Finnish is a Uralic language widely regarded as hard, so long-term settlement carries a real, escalating language barrier.
evidence: EF EPI 2025: Finland score 603, ranked 12th of 123, 'very high proficiency'.
English widely used in tech workplaces and English-taught master's programmes.
A2 Finnish/Swedish for citizenship; B1 for permanent residence.
Finnish (Uralic) is widely considered difficult for newcomers, raising the integration bar.

links.title: EF English Proficiency Index 2025 (report PDF)
Migri – Period of residence (language requirement)

safety-law.summary: World-leading rule of law (WJP 2025 #3) and second-least-corrupt (CPI 2025, score 88); very low crime.
safety-law.reasoning: Finland sits at the very top globally for rule of law and integrity, ranking 3rd in the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 (behind Denmark and Norway) and 2nd least corrupt on Transparency International's 2025 CPI with a score of 88. Strong legal protections, order and security make it among the safest destinations, though migrants face administrative scrutiny under the current policy climate.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: Finland ranked 3rd globally (Denmark 1, Norway 2).
Transparency International CPI 2025: score 88, 2nd of 180 countries.
Strong order/security, fundamental rights and civil/criminal justice scores.
Consistently rated among the world's safest, least-corrupt societies.

links.title: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 – global press release
Transparency International CPI 2025 – Finland

politics.summary: Stable institutions but a right-wing Orpo coalition with the Finns Party drives a sustained immigration crackdown.
politics.reasoning: Institutions are stable, but the Orpo coalition (in office since 2023) includes the radical-right Finns Party as the main driver of an unprecedented immigration crackdown, having tightened citizenship, PR and work-permit rules, added a citizenship test and cut refugee intake across 2024-2026. The restrictive, anti-immigrant policy direction and ongoing volatility in the rules are a clear negative for a non-EU Muslim couple's long-term certainty.
evidence: Orpo coalition (since 2023): National Coalition + Finns Party (20.1% in 2023) + SPP + Christian Democrats.
Government declared a 'paradigm shift' immigration crackdown.
Citizenship, PR and work-permit conditions tightened repeatedly in 2024-2026; citizenship test added.
Refugee resettlement quota cut; eastern-border asylum rejection law extended to end-2026.

links.title: Ministry of the Interior – Government Programme migration measures
Finland: domestic politics and the Orpo Government since 2023 (UK Commons Library)

tax.summary: Effective ~30% on a €60k Helsinki salary (~70% net); Key Employee 25% flat regime can help high earners.
tax.reasoning: Finland's burden is high-but-not-extreme: a €60,000 Helsinki engineer faces roughly 30% combined state and municipal income tax (~5.8%) plus ~8.65% social contributions, taking home about 70% — comparable to Germany and lighter than Sweden or Denmark only at the margin. Foreign experts can elect the Key Employee flat 25% regime for up to 84 months if earning ≥€5,800/month, modestly improving take-home, but Finland remains well above low-tax destinations.
evidence: €60,000 Helsinki salary: ~30% effective rate, ~70% net (2026).
Employee social contributions 8.65% (pension 7.15%, unemployment 0.79%, health 0.84%) in 2026.
Municipal tax: Helsinki ~5.3-5.8%; national average ~7.57%.
Key Employee flat 25% regime for foreign experts earning ≥€5,800/month, up to 84 months.

links.title: OECD – Taxing Wages 2026: Finland
Finland – Individual taxes on personal income (PwC)

muslim-diaspora.summary: Small Muslim community (~3%), modest Bangladeshi presence; mosques/halal in cities but Finland #3 in EU for Muslim discrimination (63%).
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Finland's Muslim population is small (~150,000-170,000, ~2.7-3%), concentrated in Greater Helsinki, with a modest Bangladeshi/Sunni Bengali community and mosques plus halal food in major cities (e.g. Helsinki Mosque, Helsinki Islamic Centre). However, an EU FRA survey ranks Finland 3rd-highest in the EU for anti-Muslim discrimination (63% of Muslims report it, behind Austria and Germany) and Islamophobia is rising, a real concern for a visibly Muslim South Asian couple.
evidence: Muslim population ~150,000-170,000 (~2.7-3%), concentrated in Uusimaa/Greater Helsinki.
Mosques and halal food available in Helsinki, Tampere and Turku; Bangladeshi community modest.
EU FRA: Finland 3rd-highest in EU for Muslim discrimination (63%), after Austria (71%) and Germany (68%).
Islamophobia rising; surveys show notably negative attitudes toward Muslim communities.

links.title: Euronews – Islamophobia rising across Europe (FRA data)
Helsinki Times – negative attitudes toward Muslim communities in Finland
